text:
Aussie swimmer Ariarne Titmus has lifted the lid on the living conditions of the Olympic Village, including the lengths athletes would go to for toilet paper supplies which left hosts of The Project in shock.

Champion Aussie swimmer Ariarne Titmus has lifted the lid on the living conditions of the Olympic Village, leaving The Project hosts in shock upon hearing the revelations.

Athletes have been divided over the state of this year's Olympic Village in Paris, many not pleased afterdiscovering the eco-friendly cardboard bedshad made a comeback from Tokyo.

Speaking to Network Ten's The Project on Thursday night, Titmus admitted the living arrangements were not "as glamorous as people think".

"Our bathroom in my apartment was bigger than our living room for the four of us," Titmus told the panel.

"Our bed sheets got changed I think after first night we were there, and then they didn’t get changed for the rest of the time we were there, so we were like living in filth."

The hosts appeared to stare at the gold medallist with disbelief as she hilariously confessed the lengths athletes would go to in order to fetch more toilet paper.

"Another thing was we would have to lie about how being roommates we so we could scab toilet rolls," Titmus said.

"We would run out of toilet roll and then they’d give us one for four days for the entire apartment."

Host Waleed Aly joked it reminded him of the scenes in supermarkets during the pandemic.

"This is like the start of Covid," Aly said.

But there were some highlights of the living quarters for the Aussie team, Titmus explained, as she pointed to the great coffee service.

"I think the best part was our baristas, we had two baristas down the bottom who probably worked the hardest in the little Aussie precinct," the swimmer said.

"I was excited to go down every morning and get a coffee."

Titmus, who was one of more than Aussie 460 athletes to compete at the games, said the team was "so looked after" by the Australian Olympic Committee.

"They did it all up and it felt like home. We had Aussie food, Tim Tams, choccies, everything," she added.

Titmus took home agold medal in the women's 400m freestyle event, as well as a first placing in the 4 x 200 metre freestyle relay with her fellow Aussie swimmers.

Her comments echoed other international athletes, including legendary American gymnast Simone Biles, who also expressed their confusion at the living conditions of the Olympic Village.

Biles was just one competitor to share humorous reviews of the infamous cardboard beds on social media.

"The bed sucks… but we are getting mattress toppers so hopefully it’ll get better," Biles commented on a TikTok video.

British diver Tom Daley also shared a clip showing fans the details of the mattress.

"As you can see, they are pretty sturdy," he said, jumping about on the single bed.

title: 'Would have to lie': Aussie gold medal swimmer Ariarne Titmus lifts lid on shock living conditions of Olympic Village in Paris | Sky News Australia
description: Champion Aussie swimmer Ariarne Titmus has lifted the lid on the living conditions of the Olympic Village, leaving The Project hosts in shock upon hearing the revelations.
